finding: §9.1 hashes each role's 'canonical (id, title, instructions, focus, domains)' but never defines the canonical byte serialization fed to sha256: field order, separators, whitespace, encoding, and — critically for eject-neutrality — how an absent optional focus/domains (§3.3: empty/absent = all domains) is normalized versus an explicit []. Eject-neutrality (§5.4/§9.1) requires the on-disk ejected JSON to canonicalize to the SAME tuple as the embedded default, but whether --eject-roles writes domains explicitly (e.g. ["software"]) or omits it, and whether canonicalization treats nil focus == [] focus, is unspecified. I cannot write the headline test 'eject the default corpus, recompute review_roles_hash, assert it equals the pre-eject embedded hash' — two conforming implementations can emit different bytes for the same logical role.
suggestion: Specify the exact canonical form (e.g. JSON with keys in fixed order id,title,instructions,focus,domains; absent focus/domains normalized to []; no insignificant whitespace; UTF-8) and mandate that --eject-roles emits that same normalized shape so embedded and ejected roles hash identically.

finding: §8.5 says `--merge`, `--report`, and `--status` all report 'the round's overlap ratio' and mechanize candidates, but: (a) `--report` spans multiple recorded rounds — per-round or aggregate overlap?; (b) `--status` is not round-scoped — which round's overlap ratio (latest recorded)?; (c) the existing class-frequency signal is cross-round in current code (rounds_seen>=2 OR >=5-in-one-round), but `--merge` sees a single round, so the 'union with existing class-frequency candidates' has no cross-round leg there; (d) the union 'annotated with which signals fired' defines no output field/shape, and when several clusters share one class with different `found_by`, the merged candidate's `found_by`/`total` value is undefined.
suggestion: Define, per command, the exact round scope of the overlap ratio; state whether class-frequency counts raw findings or post-merge clusters; and pin the merged mechanize-candidate object schema (e.g. {class, signals:[overlap|frequency], found_by=max over that class's clusters, rounds_seen, total}).

finding: §9.1 hashes 'that phase's active roles', and §6.2 defines the 'active' panel as the domain-FILTERED set. If 'active' means domain-filtered, editing the spec's tp.domain frontmatter changes which roles are active and therefore changes review_roles_hash/audit_roles_hash, flipping roles_stale (§9.4) in addition to the spec stale flag. §9.3 explicitly avoids double-counting the frontmatter override but says nothing about domain. If 'active' means the full phase corpus, a domain edit leaves the hash unchanged. The two readings yield contradictory tests: 'change tp.domain, assert roles_stale unchanged' vs '... assert roles_stale=true'.
suggestion: State whether the roles-hash covers the full phase corpus (domain-independent) or the domain-filtered active set, and reconcile with §9.3's no-double-count intent (recommend hashing the full corpus so domain lives only in spec_hash).

finding: §8.2 says the clustering key is 'the exact stamped anchor — a §<n>(.<n>)* token'. For a location containing a § token plus trailing text (e.g. '§8.2 Finding Dedup') it is undefined whether the key is the EXTRACTED token '§8.2' or the full stamped string, so '§8.2' and '§8.2 Finding Dedup' may or may not cluster. Worse, a location with MULTIPLE tokens ('§8.2 and §8.3', common when a reviewer cites two sections) has no rule for which token keys the cluster (first? all? none?). I cannot deterministically write 'finding at §8.2 and finding at "§8.2 Dedup", equal class, cluster together: yes or no'.
suggestion: Define token extraction precisely, e.g. 'the key is the FIRST §<n>(.<n>)* match in location with trailing text ignored; a location with no token uses the byte-identical string', and specify multi-token behavior (reject at emission, or key on first).

finding: §9.4 says a pre-v0.25.0 state.json with no stored role hash 'treats the current hash as the baseline (no retroactive staleness) on first read', but does not say whether that first read (e.g. tp review --status, historically read-only) PERSISTS the baseline into state.json. If it persists, editing a role after the first status read marks roles_stale; if it does not (baseline only written at --record), repeated status reads never establish a baseline and an edited role stays non-stale until the next record. §9.2 says the hashes 'are stored' but not when. The test 'pre-0.25 state → tp review --status → edit a reviewer → tp review --status → roles_stale?' has no determinable expected value.
suggestion: State the write point explicitly, e.g. 'the baseline hash is persisted only by --record; --status is read-only and, when no hash is stored, reports roles_stale=false by treating current==baseline'.

finding: The (location, class) clustering + overlap/mechanize pipeline (§8) is written entirely for `tp review --merge`; there is no `tp audit --merge` command and audit --record explicitly carries no mechanize output. Yet §7.3 mandates that AUDIT prompts also stamp `role` and `location` 'to make dedup and overlap reporting possible,' and Principle 1 claims the dedup pipeline is 'phase-parameterized, not duplicated.' An implementer cannot tell whether audit findings are ever clustered or where audit overlap is reported.
suggestion: Either (a) explicitly add audit-phase clustering/overlap (a `tp audit --merge`, or fold clustering into `tp audit --record`) and state its outputs, or (b) scope §7.3's location-stamp mandate to review only and drop the Principle-1 'phase-parameterized dedup' claim for the audit phase.

finding: The merge key changes from today's (category, location, finding-text) to (location, class). §8 says a cluster records `found_by`/`found_by_roles` but never says which member supplies the cluster's representative `severity`, `category`, `finding`, and `suggestion`. Because `category` is no longer in the key, two findings with the same (location, class) but different categories (e.g. 'feasibility' vs 'ambiguity') and different severities now collapse into one row with an unspecified winner — a feasibility/critical finding can be silently hidden under an ambiguity/low label.
suggestion: Specify representative selection: keep highest severity (as the current merge does), define category/finding/suggestion tie-breaks, and state what happens when members disagree on category — either carry the highest-severity member's fields or retain a category set.

finding: §7.3 says emission instructs the sub-agent to stamp only role and location. But Principle 2's output contract requires class and severity (and audit status) on every finding, and §8.3's clustering key depends on class. Since the emitted prompt never asks for class, sub-agents will routinely omit it, and §8.3 keeps every classless finding as its own singleton cluster — so the headline (location, class) dedup degrades to location-only-when-class-happens-to-exist and the §8.5 overlap ratio is systematically deflated by unrequested-class singletons. Distinct from the prior role-validation finding: the point here is that class/severity/status are never even requested in the prompt.
suggestion: Make §7.3 instruct the sub-agent to stamp the full output contract — role, location, class, severity, and (audit) status — matching Principle 2, so the dedup key's class component is actually populated.

finding: §1 and §8.5 motivate overlap reporting as letting the orchestrator 'trim redundant roles,' but the only per-round signals §8.5 emits are a corpus-global overlap ratio and class-keyed mechanize candidates. Neither identifies WHICH role is redundant. The per-cluster found_by_roles set (§8.4) is the data that could, but it is never aggregated per-role or per-role-pair and §8.5 never surfaces it. The report-then-orchestrator-trims story is therefore incoherent: the reported signal cannot drive the trim decision it exists to enable.
suggestion: Either add a per-role overlap aggregation to §8.5 (e.g. for each role, the fraction of its findings another role independently duplicated, plus the top overlapping counterpart role) so the orchestrator can name the role to trim, or drop the 'trim redundant roles' framing from §1/§8.5 and scope overlap reporting to mechanization only.

finding: §10.3 defines effective focus as a two-layer concatenation (project-corpus + spec-override, project first). §10.4's legacy-`lens` back-compat shim introduces a THIRD contributor that also 'appends' to a role's focus. When a spec carries both a legacy `tp: lens` block and a §10.2 `review_roles` override for the same active role, the ordering among {corpus, review_roles override, lens shim} is undefined, so the emitted focus order — which affects prompt content and is order-significant — is non-deterministic across implementations.
suggestion: Extend §10.3's concatenation chain to a defined position for the translated legacy-lens focus (e.g. corpus + lens-shim + review_roles-override), or make `lens` and `review_roles`/`audit_roles` coexisting in one frontmatter a lint error.

finding: §8.3 keeps a finding with 'empty or absent' class as its own cluster, while §8.4 treats a 'missing or blank' role specially. The wording differs: is a whitespace-only class "   " 'empty' (→ own cluster, never merged) or a legitimate non-empty key (→ two such findings at the same location MERGE)? §8.4's 'blank' implies trimming; §8.3's 'empty' read literally does not. Two testers write opposite tests for 'two findings, same location, class="  ": one cluster or two?'.
suggestion: Use one normalization rule for both fields: define 'blank' = trims-to-empty and apply it identically to class (§8.3) and role (§8.4).

finding: §6.2's empty-panel fallback ('falls back to that phase's embedded default corpus') is under-defined when the spec domain is UNKNOWN and role files exist but domain-filter to empty. §6.1 resolves an unknown domain to the software corpus only 'when role files are absent'; here files are present-but-filtered-out, so which embedded corpus the fallback uses (software? error? the unknown domain's non-existent corpus?) is unspecified. Test unwritable: 'custom reviewers all domains=[prose], spec domain=martian (unknown) → which reviewer panel is emitted?'.
suggestion: Make §6.1's unknown-domain→software resolution apply before §6.2's empty-panel fallback on all paths so the fallback corpus is always well-defined; state it explicitly.

finding: §10.4 auto-translates a legacy tp: lens block into §10.2 review-role overrides, but a spec may carry BOTH a legacy tp: lens block and explicit tp.review_roles overrides for the same role. The resulting focus order is undefined: is effective focus project ⊕ lens-shim ⊕ review_roles, or project ⊕ review_roles ⊕ lens-shim, or is one ignored? §10.3 fixes only project⊕spec-override order, not shim-vs-override. Test unwritable: 'lens.tester=[Q1] plus review_roles.tester.focus=[Q2] → tester effective focus tail = [Q1,Q2] or [Q2,Q1]?'.
suggestion: Define precedence when both legacy lens and §10.2 overrides target the same role (recommend: translate lens first, then append §10.2 focus, i.e. project ⊕ lens ⊕ override), or make their coexistence a lint warning.

finding: §9.1 derives review_roles_hash over 'that phase's active roles.' Per §7.1 the active review panel includes the built-in `regression` role, but §5.2 says regression is not a corpus file and its prompt is tp's fixed built-in. The spec never says whether regression participates in review_roles_hash. If included, the hash couples to tp's built-in prompt and would flip staleness across tp upgrades even with an unchanged user corpus; if excluded, that must be stated so implementers don't include it.
suggestion: State explicitly that review_roles_hash covers the corpus reviewer roles only and excludes the built-in `regression` role (or, if it is included, acknowledge and accept the cross-version staleness that follows).

finding: §6.2's empty-panel fallback silently substitutes the embedded default corpus when a project's entire authored panel is filtered out by domain. No observable (warning, or a note in --status/--record output) is specified, so a tester cannot assert the user is informed that their authored roles were all dropped — the case is indistinguishable from 'defaults were intended'.
suggestion: Require a lint/emit warning when domain filtering empties an authored panel and the embedded fallback is used (e.g. 'all N authored reviewers excluded by domain <d>; using embedded default corpus') and expose it as a testable output field.

finding: §8.5's mechanize-candidate output is only partially specified: candidates are 'keyed on class ... annotated with which signals fired', but the record's other fields (contributing location(s), the found_by count, a class-frequency count) are undefined, and 'the existing class-frequency candidates' membership rule/threshold is referenced without being anchored in this spec. Without the class-frequency membership rule I cannot compute the union, and without the candidate field set I cannot assert on --merge/--report output. Test unwritable: 'class off-by-one at §8.2 (found_by=2) and §8.3 (found_by=2) → how many mechanize candidates, carrying what fields?'.
suggestion: Define the mechanize-candidate object schema (at minimum {class, signals[], and either a count or the contributing locations}) and restate or cite the exact class-frequency threshold used in the union.

finding: §9.1 hashes 'that phase's active roles' but §5.2 makes regression a built-in appended to every review emission that is 'not a corpus file'. It is unspecified whether regression's fixed prompt is included in review_roles_hash. If included, a future tp release that tweaks the regression prompt flips roles_stale for every project on upgrade; if excluded, it never does. The determinism test 'upgrade tp with a changed regression prompt, recompute review_roles_hash, assert unchanged' has no defined expected result.
suggestion: State explicitly that the built-in regression role is excluded from review_roles_hash (the hash covers only user-visible/editable corpus roles), so upgrades do not spuriously invalidate review convergence.

finding: §9.2 says the role hashes are 'stored in state.json' but never states at which command they are written. §9.4's pre-v0.25.0 rule ('treats the current hash as the baseline on first read') implies a read computes-and-compares, but if that baseline is persisted on read, a role edit made before the first --status is silently masked (the baseline captures the post-edit hash). If instead hashes are written only at --record (parallel to spec_hash), that must be stated.
suggestion: Specify the write point: role hashes are recorded to state.json only at --record (mirroring spec_hash); --status reads and compares without persisting. Make §9.4's 'baseline on first read' a non-persisting comparison so a pre-status edit is not swallowed.

finding: §9.1 hashes 'the effective role set's content,' including each role's `focus`; but §10.3 defines a role's 'effective focus' as project-corpus focus + spec-override focus, and §9.3 says the spec-override is covered by `spec_hash` and is 'not counted in the role hashes.' So the `focus` hashed in §9.1 must be the CORPUS focus, not the override-merged 'effective' focus — yet §9.1 uses the word 'effective.' The overloaded term leaves it ambiguous whether override focus enters review_roles_hash (and thus whether editing an override double-counts).
suggestion: In §9.1 replace 'the effective role set's content' with 'the corpus role set's content (before spec-frontmatter overrides)' and cross-reference §9.3 so the hash input is unambiguous.

finding: §8.2 clusters on the EXACT stamped §<n> anchor with 'no fuzzy heading lookup.' Two reviewers who flag the same defect at different citation depths — one stamps §8, another §8.2 — produce non-equal keys and never merge, though it is one duplicate. Reviewers naturally cite at different granularity, so exact-anchor matching deflates the overlap ratio and inflates cluster count, eroding the very signal §8.5 reports and §1 motivates. This is separate from the prior no-§-token normalization findings: both anchors here are valid §-tokens and still fail to merge.
suggestion: Either normalize anchors to a canonical depth for the cluster key (e.g. cluster by prefix containment / the leading §<n> component), or acknowledge and bound the limitation in §8.2 and have §7.3 instruct reviewers to cite at the leaf subsection so depths align.

finding: §6.2's empty-panel fallback ('if domain filtering leaves a phase's active panel empty, tp falls back to that phase's embedded default corpus') does not say whether the embedded fallback roles are themselves subjected to the same `domains` filter that just produced the empty panel. Since ejected/embedded defaults are byte-equivalent role files that carry `domains` (§5.4), applying the filter could yield another empty set, whereas skipping it means embedded roles bypass a filter user roles must obey — an asymmetry the 'never reviews with zero roles' guarantee does not resolve.
suggestion: State that the embedded default corpus for the resolved domain (§6.1) is emitted unfiltered — it is already domain-selected, so the fallback is guaranteed non-empty — and cross-reference §6.1's domain resolution.

finding: §8.1 changes --merge clustering from literal-text similarity to (location, class), which assumes findings carry stamped §-anchors and classes. §13 (Migration) covers state.json hashes and .tp-active but not in-flight review/audit findings recorded under v0.24.0, which lack stamped location/class. A project mid-loop that upgrades will see those un-stamped findings cluster as singletons. Impact is low because merge is per-round and each new round re-runs sub-agents under the §7.3 prompts, but the migration section never mentions it.
suggestion: Add one line to §13: pre-v0.25.0 recorded findings lacking stamped location/class cluster as singletons on the first post-upgrade merge and self-heal on the next freshly emitted round — confirming the gap is intentional and bounded.

finding: §10.4 makes `lens.all` the sentinel meaning 'every active review role,' and `lens.<role-id>` target one role. But role ids match `^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$`, so `all` is a legal role id (a file `all.json`). A project with a role literally named `all` cannot address it via the shim and cannot tell whether `lens.all` means the sentinel or that role — the same reserved-word hazard §3.2 closed for `regression` is left open for `all`.
suggestion: Either reserve the id `all` (validation error for a role file named all.json), or state that `lens.all` always denotes the sentinel and a role id `all` is intentionally unaddressable via the legacy shim.

finding: The same spec-frontmatter role-override concept is cited at two different granularities. §5.2 ("A spec-frontmatter override (§10)") and §9.3 ("A spec-frontmatter role override (§10)") point at the whole section §10, while §10.1 item 3 and §12.6 cite the precise subsection §10.2 where the override is actually defined. Both resolve (no broken link from the renumber), but the inconsistent precision makes reference-tracing noisier.
suggestion: Normalize §5.2 and §9.3 to cite §10.2 (the override definition), matching §10.1 and §12.6.

finding: Given the motivation-signal-mismatch finding and Principle 4 ('no automation that a report plus a human decision covers; every kept surface is a maintenance cost'), the scalar overlap ratio itself may be unearned: tp takes no action on it and it cannot identify a role to trim. The independently-justified part of §8.5 is the mechanize-candidate union; the ratio adds a maintained number that no downstream decision consumes.
suggestion: Under ruthless minimalism, consider cutting the aggregate overlap ratio and exposing only per-cluster found_by / found_by_roles (raw, decision-neutral) plus the mechanize-candidate union; or, if kept, tie the ratio to a concrete orchestrator decision per the motivation-signal-mismatch fix.

finding: §12.4's parenthetical '(removing the need for a boolean workflow field entirely)' references a boolean workflow toggle that this spec never introduces — a vestige of the removed auto-taper design. It reads as a justification for deleting something that is not present, which will confuse a reader diffing against the current spec.
suggestion: Delete the parenthetical (or rephrase to 'v0.25.0 introduces no workflow config field'); do not reference the removed auto-taper's would-be toggle.

finding: §12.4's parenthetical "(removing the need for a boolean workflow field entirely)" is a surviving tail of the removed auto-taper/Configuration-Surface machinery. No boolean workflow field is proposed anywhere in the v3 spec, so the phrase references a phantom the reader has no antecedent for — the toggle it justifies away (an auto-taper enable/disable knob) was deleted along with old §8 and §13.
suggestion: Drop the parenthetical, or restate it self-containedly, e.g. "— overlap is report-only (§8.5), so no config knob is introduced."
